narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated a 68.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. The primary reason for this prediction centers on your academic performance in school, particularly your lower rankings in earlier and later years compared to typical students. Your Year 3 decile ranking was the most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure—it was quite low at the 2nd decile, well below the average of around the 5th or 6th, signaling to the model a concerning dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 37, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction slightly toward passing, as it's right around the typical score of 36.7 and shows solid standardized reasoning ability. Your Year 1 decile of 3rd place also contributed toward the failure prediction, though less strongly than Year 3, since it too falls short of the average 5th or 6th decile and highlights early academic challenges. Other aspects like your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 (below the 3.21 average), full-time student status (common among 93% of applicants), and family income didn't sway the decision as much. Compared to students who typically pass, your profile shows weaker high school deciles that stand out as red flags, even with strengths like your LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements—but the model prioritizes patterns from historical data. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT if possible to boost it further, addressing any gaps in your academic record through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and consider schools with more holistic reviews. Building a stronger narrative around your Year 3 performance could also help in appeals or re-applications. With persistence, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
